coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped MoS2 QDs as fluorescent probes for sensitive detection of curcumin and cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Jia
Zhai, Zhiyao
Chen, Yuru
Li, Zhaohui
Yang, Hongfen
Cai, Ren
coredata.dc:description:
Background Curcumin has been used in traditional medicine because of its pharmacological activity, including antioxidant, antibacterial, anticancer, and anticarcinogenic properties. Therefore, sensitive and selective monitoring of curcumin is highly demand for practical application.
Results In this study, we describe the construction of a fluorescence method for curcumin assay based on nitrogen-doped MoS2 quantum dots (N–MoS2 QDs). The N–MoS2 QDs are constructed by a solvothermal method using sodium molybdate and Cys as precursors. With the addition of curcumin, the bright blue fluorescence of N–MoS2 QDs is quenched by the inner filter effect (IFE). The QDs emitted bright blue fluorescence and could be quenched by the addition of curcumin via IFE. The dynamic range is the range of 0.1–10 μM for curcumin detection, with a detection limit of 59 nM. N–MoS2 QDs were applied for curcumin assay in real samples with good recovery. In addition, the N–MoS2 QDs exhibited relative low cytotoxicity and could be applied for fluorescence-based imaging in biological samples.
Significance Our study indicates that the sensor possesses good selectivity to monitor curcumin in water samples, human urine samples, ginger powder samples, mustard samples, and curry samples with satisfactory recoveries. The N–MoS2 QDs possess less cytotoxicity with excellent biocompatibility and were applied for in vitro cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Curcumin
Nitrogen-doped MoS2 QDs
Inner filter effect
Fluorescence
Cell imaging

Cytotoxicity assay and application in cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) histogram of the size distribution of the N–MoS2 QDs. (C–F) High resolution XPS spectra of the N–MoS2 QDs: (C) full range, (D) N 1s, (E) Mo 3d, and (F) S 2p.
(A) FL excitation (a) and emission spectra (b) of N–MoS2 QDs. (B) FL emission spectra of MoS2 QDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(A) FL spectra of different systems: N–MoS2 QDs (curve a) and N–MoS2 QDs + curcumin (curve b). (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin (curve a) and FL excitation spectrum of N–MoS2 QDs (curve b).
(A) FL of N–MoS2 QDs in the presence of different curcumin concentrations from 0 to 20 μM. (B) Calibration curve for curcumin.

Interference of (A) Na+, K+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+ and (B) Phe, Arg, Met, Thr, Ala, His, Leu, Ser, Asp, Glu, Cys, Gly, Val, Tyr, urease, glucose, lactose, ALP, GOx on the detection of curcumin. The concentration of curcumin was 20 μM; the concentration of Na+, K+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Phe, Arg, Met, Thr, Ala, His, Leu, Ser, Asp, Glu, Cys, Gly, Val, Tyr, glucose, lactose was 200 μM; the concentration of urease, ALP, GOx was 10 U mL−1.
HeLa Cell viability analysis after incubation with different concentrations of N–MoS2 QDs.
Confocal images of N–MoS2 QDs incubated with HeLa cells at different incubation time.
Illustration of the sensing strategy for monitoring curcumin based on nitrogen-doped MoS2 QDs.
Comparison with other detection methods for curcumin determination.
Performance of curcumin detection in water samples and human urine samples.
Performance of curcumin detection in ginger powder samples, mustard samples, and curry powder samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped MoS2 QDs as fluorescent probes for sensitive detection of curcumin and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory of Typical Environmental Pollution and Health Hazards, School of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang, 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Typical Environmental Pollution and Health Hazards

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Key Laboratory of Typical Environmental Pollution and Health Hazards, School of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin has been used in traditional medicine because of its pharmacological activity, including antioxidant, antibacterial, anticancer, and anticarcinogenic properties. Therefore, sensitive and selective monitoring of curcumin is highly demand for practical application.

In this study, we describe the construction of a fluorescence method for curcumin assay based on nitrogen-doped MoS2 quantum dots (N–MoS2 QDs). The N–MoS2 QDs are constructed by a solvothermal method using sodium molybdate and Cys as precursors. With the addition of curcumin, the bright blue fluorescence of N–MoS2 QDs is quenched by the inner filter effect (IFE). The QDs emitted bright blue fluorescence and could be quenched by the addition of curcumin via IFE. The dynamic range is the range of 0.1–10 μM for curcumin detection, with a detection limit of 59 nM. N–MoS2 QDs were applied for curcumin assay in real samples with good recovery. In addition, the N–MoS2 QDs exhibited relative low cytotoxicity and could be applied for fluorescence-based imaging in biological samples.

Our study indicates that the sensor possesses good selectivity to monitor curcumin in water samples, human urine samples, ginger powder samples, mustard samples, and curry samples with satisfactory recoveries. The N–MoS2 QDs possess less cytotoxicity with excellent biocompatibility and were applied for in vitro cell imaging.

A new label-free nanosensor for curcumin assay has been developed based on nitrogen-doped MoS2 quantum dots quenched via inner filter effect.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin is a natural polyphenolic pigment derived from Curcuma longa [1,2]. In recent years, curcumin has been used in traditional medicine because of its pharmacological activity, including antioxidant, antibacterial, anticancer, and anticarcinogenic properties [3–6]. Also, curcumin is recognized as a natural additive for food and cosmetics by the World Health Organization (WHO) [7]. Therefore, it is crucial to properly control the concentration of curcumin in food and medicine. Currently, some measurement strategies, including electrochemical methods [8], high performance liquid chromatography [9], and liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry [10], have been developed for detection of curcumin. However, the shortcomings, such as the need for expensive and sophisticated instruments, complicated operation procedures, and long analysis time, have hampered their further application to some extent. Therefore, development of a sensitive and simple method to overcome the above limitations is still appealing.

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is a member of both the transition metal dichalcogenide families [11,12]. MoS2 quantum dots (QDs) exhibit desirable features, such as tunable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, and high light stability, can be applicable in fluorescence sensing, catalysis, energy storage, and bioimaging [13–16]. Based on MoS2 quantum dots QDs, the development of fluorescent probes has been used for the detection of target analytes, including inorganic ions, organic explosive residues, and biomolecules [17–22]. However, there is no report on employing MoS2 quantum dots QDs-based methods for curcumin detection.

Herein, we report a sensitive curcumin detection method using N–MoS2 QDs as the nanoprobe. N–MoS2 QDs were constructed by a solvothermal method using sodium molybdate and Cys as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The QDs emit bright blue fluorescence, which can be quenched by the addition of curcumin via the inner field effect (IFE). Based on this principle, a sensitive and simple analytical method was designed for curcumin assay, and was effectively utilized for curcumin assay in the real samples.

0.145 g sodium molybdate was dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water, and the solution was adjusted to pH = 6.5 with HCl (0.2 M). Then, 0.5 g cysteine was dropped into the solution under ultrasonication, and fully stirred at 30 °C. The reaction was then conducted for 24 h at 180 °C in a Teflon autoclave. After cooling to 25 °C, the solution was dialyzed with a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The N–MoS2 QDs were obtained after dialysis.

First, 20 μL the N–MoS2 QDs solution (0.4 mg/mL), 70 μL PBS buffer solution (pH 7.2), and 10 μL of different concentrations of curcumin were added to a 2 mL centrifuge tube, which was incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, the fluorescence was measured. Curcumin and possible interferents, including metal ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+), phenylalanine, arginine, methionine, threonine, alanine, histidine, leucine, serine, aspartic acid, glutamic acid, cysteine, glycine, valine, tyrosine, urease, glucose, lactose, alkaline phosphatase, and glucose oxidase were added to the N–MoS2 QDs solution to measure their fluorescence spectra for selectivity testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of curcumin in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed strategy for detection of curcumin was achieved using the standard addition method to detect curcumin in water and human urine samples. The water samples came from Mei Lake, Zhengzhou University. The urine sample came from the healthy people. All types of samples were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the subsequent analysis was similar to the detection of curcumin in buffer solution.

The morphology and size of N–MoS2 QDs were characterized by TEM. The MoS2 QDs exhibited the uniform distribution with an average diameter of 2.0 ± 0.6 nm, which was average of at least 100 particles in TEM images (Fig. 1 A and B). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was used to characterized the chemical states of the QDs. Fig. 1C displays the peaks at 166.5, 399.3 and 232.3 eV, which were assigned to the S 2p, N1s, and Mo3d electrons, respectively. N1s spectrum can be deconvolved into two peaks at 397.5 eV and 399.7 eV, relevant to Mo–N and N–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 1D). The Mo3d spectrum exhibited two characteristic peaks, indicating the presence of Mo 3d5/2 (232.05 eV) and Mo 3d3/2 (235.15 eV) (Fig. 1E). The S 2p spectrum exhibited two characteristic peaks at 165.4 eV and 168.0 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 1F). The FTIR spectrum of N–MoS2 QDs exhibited two characteristic absorptions at 3267 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1 (Fig. S1), assigned to the O–H and N–H, respectively.

The N–MoS2 QDs show an emission maximum at 422 nm with excitation wavelength at 320 nm, exhibiting a large Stokes shift of 102 nm (Fig. 2 A). The excitation-dependent emission phenomenon of QDs was observed in the excitation wavelength range from 320 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 2B). The optical stability of N–MoS2 QDs was studied under different conditions. The as-obtained N–MoS2 QDs exhibited good salt stability, and the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged with the NaCl concentration increasing from 0.1 to 1.5 mM (Fig. S2), which shows high stability against change of ionic strength in the aqueous solution. The stability of the fluorescence of N–MoS2 QDs was studied at different pH values. The FL intensity of the N–MoS2 QDs was stable with pH value increasing from 3.0 to 9.0, indicating their high pH stability (Fig. S3). The stability of the N–MoS2 QDs at different temperature was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S4, the FL intensity of the N–MoS2 QDs was stable with temperature increase from 4 °C to 50 °C, indicating the good thermal stability. When the N–MoS2 QDs solution was irradiated continuously by UV lamp over a period of 60 min, the results showed that the N–MoS2 QDs possessed good photo-bleaching resistance (Fig. S5).

The MoS2 QDs emit blue fluorescence with peak wavelength at 422 nm (Fig. 3 A, curve a). Then, upon the addition of curcumin, the FL intensity of the MoS2 QDs is significantly decreased (Fig. 3A, curve b). The overlap of the curcumin absorption spectrum with the N–MoS2 QDs excitation spectrum suggests that the FL of the N–MoS2 QDs can be quenched by curcumin via either the IFE or FRET (Fig. 3B). Compared to FRET, IFE does not cause a change in the lifetime of the fluorescent material. The FL lifetimes of MoS2 QDs without and with curcumin were measured to be 6.49 ns and 6.36 ns (Fig. S6), respectively. The lifetime of N–MoS2 QDs were was almost constant, indicating that the effect of curcumin on MoS2 QDs was the fluorescence quenching mechanism of IFE [23,24], in which the excitation wavelengths are partially absorbed before reaching the observed part of the solution.

In order to further study the selectivity, interference assays were conducted under identical conditions using various natural amino acids, metal ions, and enzymes, including Na+, K+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, phenylalanine (Phe), arginine (Arg), methionine (Met), threonine (Thr), alanine (Ala), histidine (His), leucine (Leu), serine (Ser), aspartic acid (Asp), glutamic acid (Glu), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), valine (Val), tyrosine (Tyr), urease, glucose, lactose, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), glucose oxidase (GOx). As shown in Fig. 5 A, Fe3+ had a slight fluorescent quenching effect, while other metal ions had no interference with fluorescence compared with curcumin. The results demonstrate that none of the studied interferents had an obvious effect on the detection of curcumin. As shown in Fig. 5B, considering amino acids and enzymes, these possible interferents substances exhibited little effect on the fluorescence reaction system, which can be ignored compared with curcumin. The result indicated that as-proposed method had highly selectivity for the detection of curcumin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and application in cell imaging

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, an N–MoS2 QDs based fluorescence sensing platform was designed for curcumin detection. The FL of the MoS2 QDs could be effectively quenched by curcumin owing to the IFE mechanism, with low detection limit of 59 nM. Meanwhile, the sensor possesses good selectivity to monitor curcumin in water samples, human urine samples, ginger powder samples, mustard samples, and curry samples with satisfactory recoveries. The proposed method has promising prospects for its practical application to curcumin quality monitoring. Additionally, the N–MoS2 QDs possess minimal cytotoxicity with excellent biocompatibility and can be applied for in vitro cell imaging.

Jia Ge: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zhiyao Zhai: Project administration, Methodology. Yuru Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision. Zhaohui Li: Supervision. Hongfen Yang: Writing – review & editing. Ren Cai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate Dr. Kathryn Williams for her comments during the preparation of this manuscript. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004032) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021JJ20020, 2024JJ5348).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.343090.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergistic effects of curcumin and its analogs with other bioactive compounds: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S. Hosseini-Zare, M. Sarhadi, M. Zarei, R. Thilagavathi, C. Selvam, Synergistic effects of curcumin and its analogs with other bioactive compounds: a comprehensive review. European Journal of Medicinal Chemistry. 210 (2021) 113012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Neuroprotection by curcumin: a review on brain delivery strategies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Askarizadeh, G.E. Barreto, N.C. Henney, M. Majeed, A. Sahebkar. Neuroprotection by curcumin: a review on brain delivery strategies. International Journal of Pharmaceutics. 585 (2020) 119476.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Curcumin as a multifaceted compound against human papilloma virus infection and cervical cancers: a review of chemistry, cellular, molecular, and preclinical features

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Teymouri, M. Pirro, T.P. Johnston, A. Sahebkar. Curcumin as a multifaceted compound against human papilloma virus infection and cervical cancers: a review of chemistry, cellular, molecular, and preclinical features. BioFactors. 43 (2016) 331-346.
S. Hasanzadeh, M.I. Read, A.R. Bland, M. Majeed, T. Jamialahmadi, A. Sahebkar. Curcumin: an inflammasome silencer. Pharmacological Research. 159 (2020) 104921.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A systemic review on the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of resveratrol, curcumin, and dietary nitric oxide supplementation on human cardiovascular health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Banez, M.I. Geluz, A. Chandra, T. Hamdan, O.S. Biswas, N.S. Bryan, et al. A systemic review on the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects of resveratrol, curcumin, and dietary nitric oxide supplementation on human cardiovascular health. Nutrition Research. 78 (2020) 11-26.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new approach for the microencapsulation of curcumin by a spray drying method, in order to value food products

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lucas, M. Ralaivao, B.N. Estevinho, F. Rocha. A new approach for the microencapsulation of curcumin by a spray drying method, in order to value food products. Powder Technology. 362 (2020) 428-435.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of dual-emitting (Gd,Eu)2O3- PEI@CD composite and its potential as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Guo, Z. Zhu, Z. Sun, X. Zhang, Y. Chen, Synthesis of dual-emitting (Gd,Eu)2O3- PEI@CD composite and its potential as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for curcumin, Mater. Res. Bull. 108 (2018) 83-88

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid and sensitive electrochemical detection of anticancer effects of curcumin on human glioblastoma cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.R. Suhito, W. Lee, S. Baek, D. Lee, J. Min, T.H. Kim. Rapid and sensitive electrochemical detection of anticancer effects of curcumin on human glioblastoma cells. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical. 228 (2019) 527-534.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of curcumin and piperine in biological samples by reversed-phase liquid chromatography with multi-wavelength detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.L. Rodriguez, C. Zhang, A.G. Woolfork, Z. Li, C. Bi, H. Kaur, et al. Analysis of curcumin and piperine in biological samples by reversed-phase liquid chromatography with multi-wavelength detection. Journal of Chromatography B. 1162 (2021) 112487.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First fluorescent sensor for curcumin in aqueous media based on acylhydrazone-bridged bis-tetraphenylethylene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Jiang, J. Qiu, B. Lin, H. Guo, F. Yang. First fluorescent sensor for curcumin in aqueous media based on acylhydrazone-bridged bis-tetraphenylethylene. Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy. 229 (2020) 117916.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optically active two-dimensional MoS2-based nanohybrids for various biosensing applications: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ghosh, C. J. Yang, J. Y. Lai. Optically active two-dimensional MoS2-based nanohybrids for various biosensing applications: A comprehensive review. Biosensors and Bioelectronics. 246 (2024) 115861.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel aptameric nanobiosensor based on the self-assembled DNA–MoS2 nanosheet architecture for biomolecule detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ge, E.C. Ou, R.Q. Yu, X. Chu. A novel aptameric nanobiosensor based on the self-assembled DNA-MoS2 nanosheet architecture for biomolecule detection. J Mater Chem B. 2 (2014) 625-628.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Signal-on bimodal sensing glucose based on enzyme product-etching MnO2 nanosheets for detachment of MoS2 quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Z. Chen, X. Q. Huang, X. Y. Zeng, G. T. Fang, W. J. Chen, H. B. Zhou, X. Zhong, H. L. Cai. Signal-on bimodal sensing glucose based on enzyme product-etching MnO2 nanosheets for detachment of MoS2 quantum dots. Chinese Chem. Lett. 32 (2021) 1967-1971.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence turn-on chemosensors based on surface-functionalized MoS2 quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. W. Lin, N. Dhenadhayalan, H. L. Lee, Y. T. Lin, K. C. Lin, A. Chang, Fluorescence turn-on chemosensors based on surface-functionalized MoS2 quantum dots, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical. 281 (2019) 659-669.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent MoS2 quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation, up-conversion and down-conversion bioimaging, and photodynamic therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Dong, S. Tang, Y. Hao, H. Yu, W. Dai, G. Zhao, et al. Fluorescent MoS2 quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation, up-conversion and down-conversion bioimaging, and photodynamic therapy. ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces. 8 (2016) 3107-3114.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room-temperature SO2 gas-sensing properties based on a metal-doped MoS2 nanoflower: an experimental and density functional theory investigation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhang, J. Wu, P. Li, Y. Cao. Room-temperature SO2 gas-sensing properties based on a metal-doped MoS2 nanoflower: an experimental and density functional theory investigation. Journal of Materials Chemistry A. 5 (2017) 20666-20677.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of MoS2 QDs and Eu3+ as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for point-of-care visual quantitative detection of tetracycline via smartphone-based portable platform

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, G. Shi. Rational design of MoS2 QDs and Eu3+ as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for point-of-care visual quantitative detection of tetracycline via smartphone-based portable platform. Analytica Chimica Acta. 1198 (2022) 339572.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MoS2 QDs-based sensor for measurement of fluazinam with triple signal output

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, J. Hou, S. Lan, C. Shen, D. Huo, Z. Ji, et al. MoS2 QDs-based sensor for measurement of fluazinam with triple signal output. Analytica Chimica Acta. 1108 (2020) 152-159.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: L-cystine-linked BODIPY-adsorbed monolayer MoS2 quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of biothiols based on the inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Krishna Kumar, W.B. Tseng, M.J. Wu, Y.Y. Huang, W.L. Tseng. L-cystine-linked BODIPY-adsorbed monolayer MoS2 quantum dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of biothiols based on the inner filter effect. Analytica Chimica Acta. 1113 (2020) 43-51.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel high efficient electrochemiluminescence sensor based on reductive Cu(I) particles catalyzed Zn-doped MoS2 QDs for HPV 16 DNA determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Nie, X. Zhang, Q. Zhang, Z. Liang, Q. Ma, X. Su. A novel high efficient electrochemiluminescence sensor based on reductive Cu(I) particles catalyzed Zn-doped MoS2 QDs for HPV 16 DNA determination. Biosensors and Bioelectronics. 160 (2020) 112217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent functionalized MoS2 quantum dots for heavy metal detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Schneider, M.H.M. Facure, K.B.R. Teodoro, L.A. Mercante, D.S. Correa. Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent functionalized MoS2 quantum dots for heavy metal detection. ACS Applied Nano Materials. 6 (2023) 22435-22445.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive microRNA detection by coupling nicking-enhanced rolling circle amplification with MoS2 quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ge, Y. Hu, R. Deng , Z. Li, K. Zhang, M. Shi, et al. Highly sensitive microRNA detection by coupling nicking-enhanced rolling circle amplification with MoS2 quantum dots. Analytical Chemistry. 92 (2020) 13588-13594.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inner filter effect-based fluorescent sensing systems: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chen, Y.L. Yu, J.H. Wang. Inner filter effect-based fluorescent sensing systems: a review. Analytica Chimica Acta. 999(2018) 13-26.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: S co-doped carbon dots for temperature probe and the detection of tetracycline based on the inner filter effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.X. Zhao, Y. Jiao, Z. Gao, Y.L. Yang, H. Li. N, S co-doped carbon dots for temperature probe and the detection of tetracycline based on the inner filter effect. Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry 367 (2018) 137-144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nickel chloride solution modified glassy carbon electrode for curcumin determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Zokhtareh, M. Rahimnejad. A novel sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nickel chloride solution modified glassy carbon electrode for curcumin determination. Electroanalysis. 30 (2018) 921-927.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiwalled carbon nanotube modified electrodes for the adsorptive stripping voltammetric determination and quantification of curcumin in turmeric

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Chaisiwamongkhol, K. Ngamchuea, C. Batchelor-McAuley. Multiwalled carbon nanotube modified electrodes for the adsorptive stripping voltammetric determination and quantification of curcumin in turmeric. Electroanalysis. 29 (2017) 1049-1055.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on the supramolecular interaction of curcumin and β-cyclodextrin by spectrophotometry and its analytical application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Tang, L. Ma, H. Wang. Study on the supramolecular interaction of curcumin and β-cyclodextrin by spectrophotometry and its analytical application. Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry. 50 (2002) 1355-1361.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for determination of curcumin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shi, C. Li, S. Liu. Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for determination of curcumin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. RSC Advances. 5 (2015) 64790-64796.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free sensor for curcumin determination in real sample and cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, X. Gong, W. Dong. Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free sensor for curcumin determination in real sample and cellular imaging. Talanta. 183 (2018) 61-69.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright carbon dots as fluorescence sensing agents for bacteria and curcumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.M.F. Baig, Y.C. Chen. Bright carbon dots as fluorescence sensing agents for bacteria and curcumin. Journal of Colloid and Interface Science. 501 (2017) 341-349.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: FRET-based fluorometry assay for curcumin detecting using PVP-templated Cu NCs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Yao, H. Liu, Y. Liu. FRET-based fluorometry assay for curcumin detecting using PVP-templated Cu NCs. Talanta. 223 (2021) 121741.
